entries.summary: The portfolio is positioned for a late-cycle, stagflation-adjacent regime where the Fed is pinned (28% June cut probability), the 10-year sits at 4.38%, and equities trade at 20.9x forward earnings with a negative equity risk premium — a backdrop hostile to multiple expansion and duration risk. The five picks express this view through three distinct alpha vectors: (1) AI infrastructure compounders with secular earnings momentum that can grow through macro headwinds (GOOGL, AVGO), (2) a volatility/capital-markets beneficiary that monetizes the elevated-rate-and-geopolitical environment directly (GS), (3) a defensive-growth secular compounder insulated from tariff and energy shocks (LLY), and (4) a hard-asset hedge providing negative correlation to the AI equity sleeve in a risk-off scenario (GLD). All five names carry over from prior positioning — GOOGL, AVGO, GS, LLY, and GLD — with thesis continuity confirmed by fresh Q1 2026 earnings beats across GOOGL (94% EPS upside), AVGO (106% AI revenue growth), and GS ($17.55 EPS vs. $16.47 estimate); no thesis breaks have occurred. The dominant risk being underwritten is an AI capex cycle stall or a sovereign debt shock that compresses the AI infrastructure multiple sharply — GLD and LLY serve as the structural hedges against that scenario.
entries.diversificationNote: The five picks span four distinct GICS sectors: Communication Services (GOOGL), Information Technology (AVGO), Financials (GS), Health Care (LLY), and Commodities/Real Assets (GLD) — well within the 2-per-sector hard constraint. The accepted factor concentration is that GOOGL and AVGO both have AI infrastructure exposure, but at complementary points in the value chain (cloud application layer vs. custom silicon/networking), limiting single-factor correlation risk.

entries.summary: The regime as of May 13, 2026 is confirmed late-cycle stagflation — CPI at 3.8% YoY and PPI at +6.0% YoY (both hotter than consensus), with futures now pricing just 3% odds of a rate cut in 2026 and 36% odds of a rate hike, while the S&P 500 and Nasdaq paradoxically closed at record highs driven by AI/tech momentum ignoring the inflation data. The 10-name growth book expresses this regime through four interlocking themes: AI infrastructure cash-flow leaders (GOOGL, AVGO, AMZN, META) that generate real nominal earnings in an inflationary environment; geopolitical/commodity beneficiaries (XOM, RTX) that directly monetize the Iran conflict risk premium with WTI above $104; financial sector beneficiaries of the steepening yield curve and IB recovery (GS, SPGI); and selective secular growth with pricing power insulation (LLY, VST). All seven core positions are direct carry-overs from the May 12–13 book — thesis continuity is intact with no forced rotation warranted. The only changes are META stepping down from high to medium conviction (tough Q2 comp after the 57% EPS beat) and the growth book maintaining the same fundamental structure. The 5-name defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) is carried over intact from May 12, providing stagflation hedging (GLD), quality-income ballast (SCHD, KO), optionality on dislocation (BRK.B with $380B cash), and a growth-scare duration hedge (IEF at 4.47% yield). The dominant risk being underwritten is that the AI melt-up continues to defy the macro gravity of 6% PPI and 36% rate hike odds — a 'rational bubble' scenario where earnings momentum temporarily overrides valuation concerns — which could compress the defensives underperform, a risk we accept given the 1–5 year horizon demands protection against the eventual re-rating when the inflation/rate reality reasserts.
entries.diversificationNote: The 10-name growth book spans 5 distinct GICS sectors: Communication Services (GOOGL, META — two names sharing the ad/AI monetization theme but with different moats: Search vs. Social), Information Technology (AVGO — custom silicon/ASIC), Energy (XOM — integrated oil/Iran hedge), Industrials (RTX — defense/aerospace), Health Care (LLY — GLP-1 secular), Financials (GS, SPGI — NIM/IB and data/ratings), Utilities (VST — merchant power/data center), and Consumer Discretionary (AMZN — cloud+ad, treated as IT-adjacent). The two Financials names (GS and SPGI) are accepted as sector concentration but are entirely different business models — capital markets revenues vs. recurring subscription/ratings revenue — with near-zero earnings correlation. The defensive sleeve spans four distinct exposures: gold commodity (GLD), broad dividend/quality income equity (SCHD), diversified conglomerate with embedded cash optionality (BRK.B as consumer-staples/insurance/energy proxy), intermediate Treasury duration (IEF), and consumer staples single-name (KO) — explicitly no double-up on any single exposure per construction rules.

The 10-pick growth book expresses the dominant regime themes — AI infrastructure compounding (GOOGL, AVBO, ANET), stagflation beneficiaries with pricing power (XOM, SPGI, LLY), defense/geopolitical hedge (RTX), financials benefiting from the steepening curve and M&A revival (GS), and the AI power demand supercycle (VST) — while AMZN captures the AWS re-acceleration. The dominant risk being underwritten is that the Trump-Xi summit fails to deliver on export control relaxation for AI chips, the Iran war re-escalates, and a stagflation-into-recession outcome forces simultaneous equity multiple compression and credit spread widening.

The 10 growth picks express conviction in the secular winners of this regime: AI infrastructure and cloud (GOOGL, AVGO, META, NVDA), inflation-adjacent value (XOM), rate-cycle beneficiaries (GS, SPGI), defensive secular growth (LLY), geopolitical premium (RTX), and AI power infrastructure (VST) — collectively spanning Communication Services, Information Technology, Energy, Financials, Health Care, Industrials, and Utilities across at least 6 distinct GICS sectors. entries.summary: The portfolio is positioned for a late-cycle / pre-contraction regime with a geopolitical overlay — sticky inflation at 2.8% core CPI, a frozen Fed under new Chair Warsh, and Middle East conflict energy costs all argue for quality cash flows over duration and speculative growth. The 10-name growth book concentrates in the three highest-conviction secular themes: AI infrastructure (GOOGL, AVGO, NVDA, AMZN — differentiated across search/cloud, custom silicon, GPU, and hyperscale), defense/geopolitical spending (RTX), financials cycle (GS, SPGI), healthcare compounders (LLY), energy hedge (XOM), and AI power demand (VST). All seven core carries from the prior week are maintained — GOOGL, AVGO, RTX, GS, LLY, SPGI, XOM/VST — with NVDA upgraded to high-weight (rank 3) post its clean May 20 earnings beat and AMZN re-entered at rank 10 following Ackman's Q1 accumulation and AWS acceleration to +28%. The dominant risk being underwritten is a fiscal dominance / US sovereign re-rating event where both long bonds and equities re-price simultaneously — mitigated by GLD as the primary hedge, IEF sized small for crash-scenario carry, SCHD for quality income, BRK.B as a cash-option compounder, and KO as the consumer staples anchor.
entries.diversificationNote: Growth book spans 6 distinct GICS sectors: Communication Services (GOOGL), Information Technology (AVGO, NVDA — differentiated across custom ASIC and GPU silicon), Industrials (RTX), Financials (GS, SPGI), Health Care (LLY), Energy (XOM), Utilities (VST), and Consumer Discretionary (AMZN); the two IT names share secular AI exposure but operate at different points in the value chain (networking/custom silicon vs. training/inference GPU). Defensive sleeve spans four exposures: gold (GLD), broad dividend/quality income (SCHD), diversified insurance/equity compounder (BRK.B), intermediate treasuries (IEF), and consumer staples (KO) — no exposure is doubled up.

entries.summary: The portfolio is positioned for a late-cycle stagflationary regime — real rates modestly above neutral, CPI re-accelerating toward 4%, payroll growth averaging ~48K/month in recent months (per Fed Governor Waller), and a geopolitical wildcard in Iran that is now moving toward potential resolution as S&P 500 futures surge 1%+ on Memorial Day. The growth book concentrates highest weight in the AI infrastructure compounders (GOOGL, AVGO, NVDA, AMZN) where Q1 earnings have definitively answered the monetization question — GOOGL at 82% EPS growth, NVDA at $81.6B revenue with Q2 guided to $91B, and three separate hedge fund managers (Tepper, Druckenmiller, Ackman) independently converging on AMZN as the best risk/reward in the group — while adding GS and SPGI for financial sector earnings leverage to the M&A/capital markets recovery, RTX for the geopolitical defense bid, XOM as the energy value anchor at 10x forward P/E, MRK as a beaten-down healthcare re-rating with imminent Phase 3 catalysts, and VST at medium conviction for AI power demand. The defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) spans gold, quality dividend equity, the Berkshire optionality fortress, intermediate Treasuries, and a global consumer staple — collectively providing macro hedge against fiscal stress, inflation shock, and growth slowdown simultaneously. Carry-overs: GOOGL (#1 for 7 days), AVGO (#2 for 7 days), RTX (#4-7 range for 7 days), GS (consistent top-6 for 7 days), XOM (consistent top-8 for 7 days), SPGI (consistent top-8 for 7 days), VST (consistent for 7 days), and all 5 defensives maintained; changes include META removed (thesis intact but NVDA restored to #3 high conviction post-earnings confirmation and MRK added as a new contrarian healthcare value idea given May 22 Phase 3 catalyst). The dominant risk being underwritten is the Iran peace deal closing faster than expected — it removes the XOM and RTX geopolitical premium while simultaneously reducing the inflationary overlay that supports gold and IEF positioning, but the core AI infrastructure thesis in names 1-5 remains entirely intact regardless of geopolitical outcome.
entries.diversificationNote: The 10 growth picks span 5 GICS sectors: Information Technology (AVGO, NVDA — complementary XPU/networking vs. GPU/data center exposure within AI infrastructure), Communication Services (GOOGL — search/cloud/AI monetization), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN — cloud-dominant, not pure retail), Financials (GS, SPGI — capital markets and ratings, distinct revenue drivers), Industrials (RTX — defense/aerospace), Energy (XOM — upstream value at 10x P/E), Utilities (VST — AI power demand), and Health Care (MRK — Keytruda/ADC pipeline re-rating); accepted factor concentration is the AI infrastructure theme running across NVDA, AVGO, GOOGL, and AMZN (4 of 10 growth names share AI capex sensitivity), deliberately offset by VST (AI power demand beneficiary, uncorrelated to GPU spending), and the 5 defensives spanning gold (GLD), quality dividend equity (SCHD), conglomerate fortress balance sheet (BRK.B), intermediate duration Treasuries (IEF), and global consumer staples (KO) — covering 5 distinct exposures with no overlapping instruments.

entries.summary: The portfolio is positioned for a late-cycle stagflationary regime — the rarest and most dangerous configuration for long-only equity — where re-accelerating core PCE (3.2% YoY), futures markets pricing Fed rate hikes, and a 3m10y spread oscillating around zero since February 2026 demand a high-conviction, diversified book with explicit downside architecture. The 10-name growth sleeve concentrates in AI infrastructure picks-and-shovels (AVGO, NVDA), AI platform monetizers with clean fundamental setups and no extension flags (GOOGL, AMZN), and cycle-diversifying exposures in Financials (GS, SPGI), Defense (RTX), Energy (XOM), Power Infrastructure (VST), and Healthcare value (MRK) — seven distinct sector exposures with the AI supercycle as the unifying secular thread. From the prior week's book, AVGO was elevated to rank 1 (from rank 2) ahead of the critical June 3 earnings event with $22B revenue guidance far above consensus; GOOGL, NVDA, AMZN, GS, RTX, XOM, SPGI, VST, and MRK are all thesis-intact carry-overs; no names were dropped. The dominant risk being underwritten is a June 17-18 FOMC hike signal from Chair Warsh that would violently reprice the entire growth sleeve — the 5-name defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) collectively hedges this scenario through gold safe-haven, dividend quality rotation, fortress balance sheet exposure, intermediate duration carry, and consumer staples inflation passthrough.
entries.diversificationNote: The growth book spans 6 distinct GICS sectors: Information Technology (AVGO, NVDA — 2 names), Communication Services (GOOGL — 1), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN — 1), Financials (GS, SPGI — 2), Industrials (RTX — 1), Energy (XOM — 1), Utilities (VST — 1), and Health Care (MRK — 1); the two IT names (AVGO, NVDA) share secular AI exposure but occupy different points in the value chain (custom silicon ASIC vs. GPU training infrastructure). The defensive sleeve spans long-duration fixed income (IEF at 7-10yr), gold (GLD), broad dividend/quality equity (SCHD), all-weather conglomerate (BRK.B), and consumer staples (KO) — no exposure is doubled up, and the sleeve collectively maintains near-zero or negative correlation to the growth book in a risk-off scenario.

entries.summary: The portfolio is constructed for a late-cycle stagflationary regime with a Cautiously Bullish bias — maintaining full equity exposure through high-quality AI infrastructure compounders (AVGO, GOOGL, NVDA, AMZN) while simultaneously hedging the three dominant tail risks: U.S. fiscal deterioration (GLD, IEF, BRK.B), Iran energy shock (XOM as explicit hedge), and AI capex cycle reversal (sector diversification into Financials/GS/SPGI, Industrials/RTX, Healthcare/MRK, and Utilities/VST). The growth book carries over eight of the last ten positions with only minor rank adjustments — AVGO moves to #1 given the imminent June 3 earnings catalyst with a +47% YoY revenue guide and 10.65% implied options move; LLY is rotated out in favor of MRK on relative valuation grounds within Healthcare, as MRK trades at 12–14x forward earnings vs. LLY's premium multiple, providing better asymmetry in a sector that has underperformed by 60 percentage points over 2.5 years. The dominant risk being underwritten is a hot May CPI print on June 11 combined with a hawkish FOMC surprise on June 16–17 that simultaneously compresses equity multiples, pushes the 10-year above 4.70%, and forces a reassessment of the AI capex ROI thesis — the defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) is explicitly sized to provide 25–30% portfolio buffer against this scenario.
entries.diversificationNote: The growth book spans five distinct GICS sectors: Information Technology (AVGO, NVDA — custom silicon/data center infrastructure and compute acceleration, distinct value chain positions), Communication Services (GOOGL — search/cloud/TPU), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN — cloud/logistics), Financials (GS, SPGI — capital markets and ratings duopoly, both procyclical but with different revenue drivers), Industrials (RTX — defense/aerospace), Energy (XOM — upstream/Permian), Health Care (MRK — large-cap pharma/oncology), and Utilities (VST — merchant power/nuclear AI demand); the two IT names share secular AI infrastructure exposure but occupy complementary positions in the value chain (AVGO: custom silicon/networking vs. NVDA: GPU compute); the defensive sleeve spans four distinct exposures — gold commodity hedge (GLD), broad dividend/quality income (SCHD), diversified holding company with fortress balance sheet (BRK.B), intermediate Treasury duration (IEF), and consumer staples low-beta equity (KO) — with no duplicate exposure types.

entries.summary: The portfolio operates under a late-cycle stagflationary regime — Fed on hold at 3.50-3.75% for five consecutive meetings, CPI re-accelerating to 3.8% in April with the Cleveland Fed nowcasting 4.2% for May, and the Iran conflict providing a persistent negative supply shock that compresses easing optionality. The 10 growth picks are concentrated in the AI infrastructure supercycle (AVGO, NVDA, GOOGL as the three core positions) which remains the dominant earnings revision theme despite the YTD broadening rotation, augmented by smart-money-validated compounders (AMZN, GS, SPGI) and late-cycle beneficiaries (RTX, XOM) that hedge against the real-economy rotation the June 2 update flagged as a confirmed YTD theme. Eight of the ten growth names are direct carry-overs from the prior 7-day positioning record with thesis intact; MRK replaces LLY (trimmed conviction given GLP-1 pricing headwinds) and VST is retained as the AI power demand expression in utilities. The five-name defensive sleeve spans gold inflation hedging (GLD), dividend quality equity (SCHD), all-weather balance sheet compounding (BRK.B), intermediate duration Treasury protection (IEF), and consumer staples ballast (KO). The dominant risk being underwritten is a June 11 May CPI print above 4.0% combined with a hawkish June 17 FOMC surprise under Chair Warsh — a scenario that would simultaneously compress AI multiples, push the 10-year through 4.70%, and trigger the bond vigilante fiscal credibility shock the risk officer flagged as the highest-impact tail risk.
entries.diversificationNote: The growth book spans 5 distinct GICS sectors: Information Technology (AVGO, NVDA — complementary at custom silicon vs. merchant GPU layers), Communication Services (GOOGL), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN), Financials (GS, SPGI — capital markets and financial data, not the same revenue driver), Industrials (RTX), Energy (XOM), Health Care (MRK), and Utilities (VST); the two IT names share AI infrastructure secular exposure but occupy different points in the value chain (custom XPU vs. merchant H100/Blackwell GPU), satisfying the correlation constraint. The defensive sleeve spans four exposures: gold commodity hedge (GLD), broad dividend/quality income (SCHD), all-weather equity compounder with embedded cash optionality (BRK.B), intermediate-duration Treasury hedge (IEF), and consumer staples low-beta anchor (KO) — no exposure is doubled.
